pmos

Data Warehouses — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · Cross-Functional Skills. Every dashboard you trust, every "weekly active users" number you quote in a review, every cohort chart in a board deck — they are almost never computed against the live app database. They are computed against a separate system built for one job: answering analytical questions fast over huge piles of history. That system is the data warehouse. This primer is a deep, plain-English tour for product managers who use analytics but have never seen the machinery underneath: what a warehouse is and why it is a different database from the one running your product; the OLTP versus OLAP split that explains the whole field; why warehouses store data in columns instead of rows; how analysts model the data (star schemas, facts, and dimensions); the two founding philosophies (Inmon and Kimball); the shift from ETL to ELT; the cloud warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift); the modern data stack and the rise of analytics engineering with dbt; and how warehouses differ from data lakes and lakehouses. Every term of art is defined on first use and every acronym is spelled out. Source provenance: curation-consensus across the data-engineering canon — official docs from Amazon Redshift and Google BigQuery, vendor explainers from Databricks, Snowflake-adjacent tooling (dbt, Integrate.io, Airbyte), ClickHouse's engineering writing, and practitioner references (Holistics, Astera, Simon Späti, Monte Carlo, Striim). 16 verified sources.

What a data warehouse actually is

A data warehouse is a central database built to store data from many sources and answer analytical questions over it — the kind of questions that scan months or years of history. It is not the database your product runs on. Striim describes the job plainly: data warehouses "extract data from multiple sources and transform and clean the data before loading it into the warehousing system to serve as a single source of data truth." (Striim)

The phrase "single source of truth" is the heart of it. Your signup events live in one system, your payments in another, your support tickets in a third. The warehouse pulls copies of all of them into one place, cleans them so they line up, and keeps the history. Then anyone can ask a question that spans all three without logging into three different tools.

The classic definition comes from Bill Inmon, one of the field's founders, who called a warehouse a "Subject-oriented, nonvolatile, integrated, time-variant collection of data in support of management's decisions." (Astera) Unpacked for a PM: subject-oriented means organized around business topics like customers or orders, not around app screens; integrated means data from different systems is reconciled to match; time-variant means it keeps history rather than only the current state; and nonvolatile means once data lands, it is not overwritten the way a live app constantly updates rows. That last property is why a warehouse can tell you what your metrics looked like last March.

OLTP vs OLAP: two different jobs

The single most useful idea in this whole field is that databases come in two flavors built for opposite jobs. OLTP (Online Transaction Processing) is the database behind your live product. OLAP (Online Analytical Processing) is the database behind your analytics. They are tuned for different things, and a warehouse is an OLAP system.

ClickHouse draws the line crisply: "OLTP optimises for high-frequency single-row reads and writes" while "OLAP optimises for aggregations across millions to billions of rows in sub-second time." (ClickHouse) An OLTP database is great at "add this one order," "update this one user's email," "fetch this one cart" — many small, fast operations happening at once. An OLAP database is great at "average revenue per user across every signup in the last two years" — one enormous sweep across history.

Aegis Softtech offers a memorable framing: "OLTP is the engine behind your daily transactions" — shopping carts, bank transfers, ticket bookings — while "OLAP is the brain behind your dashboards and reports." Put another way, "OLTP records the present; OLAP analyzes the past for insights." (Aegis Softtech) This is why analysts do not run their heavy queries against the production database: a query that scans millions of rows for a trend would slow down the system serving live customers. The warehouse exists so those two workloads never fight each other.

The split even drives how the data is physically arranged. AWS notes that operational queries "access most of a row's columns at once," while "data warehouse queries commonly read only a few columns for a very large number of rows." (AWS Redshift docs) That difference is worth its own section.

Why warehouses store data in columns

A normal app database stores data row by row: everything about order #1 sits together, then everything about order #2, and so on. That is perfect when you want one whole order. A warehouse flips this and stores data column by column: all the order amounts sit together, all the order dates sit together, all the customer IDs sit together. This is called columnar storage, and it is the single biggest reason warehouses are fast at analytics.

The official Redshift documentation explains why this matters: "Columnar storage for database tables is an important factor in optimizing analytic query performance, because it drastically reduces the overall disk I/O requirements." (AWS Redshift docs) I/O (Input/Output) is the work of reading data off disk — usually the slowest step. If a query needs 5 of 100 columns, a columnar store reads about five percent of the data; a row store "would read the blocks that contain the 95 unneeded columns as well." Stitch puts the design in one line: "A columnar database stores data by columns rather than by rows, which makes it suitable for analytical query processing, and thus for data warehouses." (Stitch)

Storing one column together has a second payoff: the values are all the same type and often repeat, so they compress extremely well. Airbyte describes the tricks — dictionary encoding ("replacing recurring values with compact integer tokens"), run-length encoding, and delta encoding — and notes "Compression ratios of 5–10x are common, directly saving memory and reducing cloud bills." (Airbyte) Smaller data also means the engine can use SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) processing — a hardware trick that applies one operation to thousands of values at once — to "move far less data from disk to CPU." For a PM, the takeaway is simple: warehouses are fast at "sum this across millions of rows" because of a storage choice made before a single query runs.

How the data is modeled: facts and dimensions

Raw data is messy, so analysts reshape it into a tidy pattern before people query it. The dominant pattern is the star schema. Holistics defines it as "a particular way of organizing data for analytical purposes" built from two kinds of tables. (Holistics)

A fact table "contains the primary measurements, metrics, or 'facts' of a business process" — for an e-commerce company, that is one row per order, holding the numbers you add up: amount, quantity, discount. The dimension tables "contain 'dimensions' — that is, descriptive attributes": who the customer is, what the product is called, what date it was. The dimensions "surround" the central fact table, which is why it is drawn as a star. (Holistics)

This shape is what lets a dashboard slice "revenue by region by month by product category" without rewriting the query each time — you just join the fact table to whichever dimensions you want to slice by. The philosophy, as Holistics puts it, is to "do the hard work now, to make it easy to query later." (Holistics) When your analyst says a metric is "modeled," this is roughly what they did.

The two founding philosophies: Inmon vs Kimball

There are two classic schools of thought on how to build a warehouse, and you will hear the names in any serious data conversation. This is genuinely contested ground — practitioners still debate it — so it is worth knowing both camps rather than picking a winner.

Bill Inmon's approach is top-down. Astera summarizes it: build one big, carefully structured EDW (Enterprise Data Warehouse) first, in a "normalized" form — meaning data is split into many tidy, non-repeating tables — and then derive smaller subject-specific "data marts" from it. Ralph Kimball's approach is bottom-up: start with the business questions, build dimensional star-schema data marts to answer them, and let the warehouse grow from there. As Astera notes, in Kimball's method "data marts are first formed based on the business requirements." (Astera)

Simon Späti frames the trade-off: Inmon "emphasizes a highly normalized structure" with a core "closely aligned with the source system," while Kimball "focuses on creating a Star Schema" optimized for querying. Crucially, he argues this is not a religious war — "leveraging the strengths of both Inmon and Kimball's methods can be highly effective," and the real failure is building analytics with no deliberate approach at all "(whether Kimball or Inmon)." (Simon Späti) For a PM, the practical signal is this: if your data team is debating Inmon vs Kimball, they are deciding how much up-front structure to impose before delivering answers — a real speed-versus-consistency trade-off, not bikeshedding.

ETL vs ELT: the order of operations changed

Getting data into the warehouse has its own jargon, and the acronyms look almost identical. ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) was the old way: pull data out of source systems, clean and reshape it on a separate machine, and only then load the tidy result into the warehouse. ELT (Extract, Load, Transform) is the new way: pull the raw data out and load it straight into the warehouse first, then clean and reshape it inside the warehouse using the warehouse's own power.

The order flipped because cloud warehouses got cheap and powerful. Integrate.io explains that with ELT you "load raw data directly into the data warehouse," practical now because modern warehouses "are so powerful and efficient that they can quickly and cost-effectively transform big data within the warehouse after loading." Before the cloud, doing transformation after loading "on an onsite legacy data warehouse was slow, impractical, and expensive." (Integrate.io) Alation captures the same shift in one phrase — "load raw data first, transform as needed." (Alation)

Why a PM should care: ELT means the raw data is already sitting in the warehouse, so when a metric definition changes, the team can re-transform from the raw source instead of re-running a fragile external pipeline. It makes "wait, can we redefine an active user?" a much cheaper question than it used to be.

The cloud warehouses: Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift

When people say "we use Snowflake" or "we're on BigQuery," they mean a cloud data warehouse — a warehouse you rent rather than run on your own servers. The three you will hear most are Snowflake, Google BigQuery, and Amazon Redshift. Their shared breakthrough is separating compute from storage.

In an old warehouse, the machines that stored data and the machines that ran queries were the same box, so you could not scale one without the other. Google's official docs describe BigQuery's design: its "separation of compute and storage layers lets each layer dynamically allocate resources without impacting the performance or availability of the other." (Google BigQuery docs) Airbyte notes the same of Snowflake — "separating compute and storage, enabling independent scaling." (Airbyte) In plain terms: you can throw a huge amount of computing power at one heavy query for a few minutes and then turn it off, paying only for what you used, without moving the data.

BigQuery goes a step further as "serverless," meaning there are no servers for you to manage at all. Google describes it as "a fully managed, AI-ready data platform" whose "serverless architecture lets you use languages like SQL and Python to answer your organization's biggest questions with zero infrastructure management." (Google BigQuery docs) This is the shift that made warehouses available to small teams: you no longer need a dedicated database administrator to stand one up. And because cloud warehouses are so cheap to transform inside of, they are exactly what made ELT practical (Integrate.io).

The modern data stack

The cloud warehouse rarely travels alone. It sits at the center of a set of specialized tools collectively called the modern data stack. Alation defines it as "a thoughtfully assembled collection of cloud-native tools ... that enables enterprises to ingest, store, transform, and analyze data with agility, scale, and governance." (Alation)

Airbyte lists the layers: "data ingestion, storage, transformation, analytics, reverse ETL, orchestration, observability, and governance." (Airbyte) Walking the path a number takes: an ingestion tool (Fivetran, Airbyte) copies data from your sources into the warehouse; the warehouse (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift) stores it; a transformation tool (dbt) cleans and models it; a BI tool (Looker, Tableau) — BI stands for Business Intelligence, the category of dashboard-and-reporting software — turns it into charts; and an orchestration tool (Airflow) schedules the whole chain to run on time. Observability tools watch for data going wrong, and reverse ETL pushes warehouse data back into operational tools like a CRM.

Airbyte notes the BI layer's role too: Looker's modeling language "creates a single source of truth for business metrics." (Airbyte) Alation's larger point is that this assembly moves a company "beyond 'data warehousing + BI' to a platform that supports self-service analytics" (Alation) — the world where a PM can answer their own questions instead of filing a ticket.

Transformation and analytics engineering: the rise of dbt

One tool deserves its own section because it changed how analytics teams work: dbt (data build tool). In the ELT world, the "transform" step happens inside the warehouse, and dbt is the tool most teams use to do it. Integrate.io's shorthand is exact: "dbt is the T in ELT." (Integrate.io)

dbt Labs describes the tool as bringing "the best of software engineering to the analytics workflow." Analysts "build maintainable models by writing SQL SELECT statements that reflect your business logic," and dbt runs them inside the warehouse. Critically, it adds "testing, versioning, and documentation to the world of analytics" — meaning the definition of a metric can be tested for errors, tracked in version control like real code, and documented so everyone agrees on what it means. (dbt Labs)

This gave rise to a job title you will now see everywhere: the analytics engineer, who sits between the data engineer (moves the data) and the analyst (interprets it) and owns the modeled, tested, documented tables in between. For a PM, dbt is why "what counts as an active user?" can finally have one agreed, version-controlled answer rather than five spreadsheets that quietly disagree.

Warehouse vs data lake vs lakehouse

You will hear "data lake" and "lakehouse" alongside "warehouse," and the difference comes down to when the data gets structured. Databricks defines a warehouse as a system that stores data "in a highly structured way" where data "is cleansed, transformed and integrated into a schema" before it lands — an approach called schema-on-write (decide the structure when you write the data in). A data lake, by contrast, "stores raw data in its native format" and uses schema-on-read (decide the structure later, when you read it out). (Databricks)

Monte Carlo connects each to its best use. A warehouse, with everything "cleaned, integrated, and processed before storage," is "the most sensible choice for data platforms whose primary use case is for data analysis and reporting." A data lake stores "raw data, leaving the processing part until the data is read for use," which suits "data scientists seeking to run advanced calculations and data exploration" — including machine learning, abbreviated ML. (Monte Carlo)

The lakehouse is the newer attempt to get both. Databricks describes it as combining "the scale and flexibility of a data lake with the reliability and performance of a data warehouse." (Databricks) Striim agrees it "combines the best features of both." (Striim) The simple mental model: warehouse = clean and ready for dashboards; lake = raw and flexible for data science; lakehouse = one system trying to serve both. Which one your company runs shapes how easy it is to get a tidy, trustworthy number out.

What this means for product managers

You will almost never touch the warehouse's internals, but knowing they exist changes how you work. First, it explains why analytics numbers can lag the live product: the warehouse holds a copy of data loaded on a schedule, so "today's signups" in a dashboard may be hours behind the app. That is the OLTP-versus-OLAP split (ClickHouse) showing up in your day, not a bug.

Second, it tells you who to ask for what. A metric that is "modeled" lives in a clean dimensional table an analyst built (Holistics); a question about brand-new raw data may mean it has not been modeled yet. Knowing the difference makes your asks specific instead of "can you pull some numbers."

Third, the modern stack is what makes self-service real. The whole point, in Alation's words, is to support "self-service analytics" (Alation), and serverless warehouses like BigQuery let you "answer your organization's biggest questions" (Google BigQuery docs) without infrastructure. The more you understand the layers, the more of your own questions you can answer — and the better your asks are when you do need help. And because the warehouse is the company's "single source of truth across the enterprise" (Astera), pushing your team to define metrics there, once, is how you stop the meeting where three dashboards show three different revenue numbers.

Costs, governance, and pitfalls

Warehouses are powerful, but they come with traps worth knowing. The first is cost. Because compute is rented and billed by use, a careless query that scans years of data can be genuinely expensive. This is part of why columnar storage and compression matter so much — Airbyte notes good compression is "directly saving memory and reducing cloud bills" (Airbyte). A practical habit: filter by date so your query reads a slice, not the whole history.

The second is data quality and trust. A warehouse is only a "single source of truth" if the data flowing in is correct, which is why the modern stack includes an "observability" layer (Alation) whose whole job is catching data that silently went wrong. Monte Carlo's existence as a data-observability company underscores the risk: a broken pipeline can quietly feed a confident-looking dashboard a wrong number for weeks. When a metric looks surprising, "is the pipeline healthy?" is a fair first question.

The third is governance — who can see what. Alation lists governance as a first-class layer of the modern stack (Alation), because a warehouse concentrates sensitive data from every system into one place. For a PM, that means some data you would like is locked behind access controls for good reason, and "can I get access?" is sometimes a real project, not a quick favor. The pitfall to avoid is treating the warehouse as a magic oracle: it reflects the data and definitions people put into it, and it inherits every assumption made upstream.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

This primer points outward to a handful of neighbors worth a separate look:

  • SQL fundamentals — the language you actually type to query the warehouse; everything here becomes hands-on once you can write SELECT, WHERE, GROUP BY, and JOIN.
  • Product analytics — the tools (Amplitude, Mixpanel) and event models that often sit on top of, or feed into, the warehouse.
  • Metrics design and the North Star — how to define the numbers the warehouse stores so they mean one thing across the company.
  • Experimentation and A/B testing — analyses that lean heavily on warehouse history to measure lift.
  • Data privacy and governance — the access, retention, and compliance rules that shape what can live in the warehouse and who can query it.
  • Machine learning for PMs — where the data-lake and lakehouse side of the world feeds models, the downstream cousin of analytics.

References

  1. clickhouse.com ·cited· (T1)

    ClickHouse draws the core split: "OLTP optimises for high-frequency single-row reads and writes" while "OLAP optimises for aggregations across millions to billions of rows in sub-second time." The access patterns drive the storage: OLTP systems "store rows together on disk because operational queries access most of a row's columns at once," whereas OLAP systems "store columns together on disk because analytical queries access a few columns across many rows."

  2. docs.aws.amazon.com ·cited· (T1)

    AWS's official Redshift docs explain why warehouses flip the layout: "Columnar storage for database tables is an important factor in optimizing analytic query performance, because it drastically reduces the overall disk I/O requirements." Because "data warehouse queries commonly read only a few columns for a very large number of rows," a query using 5 of 100 columns "will only need to read about five percent of the data," while a row store "would read the blocks that contain the 95 unneeded columns as well."

  3. holistics.io ·cited· (T1)

    Holistics' Analytics Setup Guidebook explains dimensional modeling: "The star schema is a particular way of organizing data for analytical purposes" built from "A fact table ... [that] contains the primary measurements, metrics, or 'facts' of a business process" and dimension tables that "contain 'dimensions' — that is, descriptive attributes." The dimension tables "surround" the fact table — hence "star" — and the whole philosophy is to "do the hard work now, to make it easy to query later."

  4. getdbt.com ·cited· (T1)

    dbt Labs describes its tool as bringing "the best of software engineering to the analytics workflow": teams "Build maintainable models by writing SQL SELECT statements that reflect your business logic," executed inside the warehouse where the data already lives. It adds "testing, versioning, and documentation to the world of analytics" — catching anomalies early, tracking every change in Git, and documenting models and columns.

  5. docs.cloud.google.com ·cited· (T1)

    Google's official docs describe BigQuery as "a fully managed, AI-ready data platform," whose "serverless architecture lets you use languages like SQL and Python to answer your organization's biggest questions with zero infrastructure management." Critically, "BigQuery's separation of compute and storage layers lets each layer dynamically allocate resources without impacting the performance or availability of the other," supporting "business intelligence, ad hoc analysis, geospatial analytics, and machine learning."

  6. astera.com ·cited· (T2)

    Astera grounds the field in its two founders. Bill Inmon's classic definition is a "Subject-oriented, nonvolatile, integrated, time-variant collection of data in support of management's decisions," built top-down as a normalized enterprise model that establishes "a single source of truth across the enterprise." Ralph Kimball works bottom-up, where "data marts are first formed based on the business requirements" and a star schema combines "a fact table with several dimensional tables."

  7. striim.com ·cited· (T2)

    Striim frames the warehouse by its job: "Data warehouses extract data from multiple sources and transform and clean the data before loading it into the warehousing system to serve as a single source of data truth." By contrast a data lake "stores large amounts of structured and unstructured data in its raw, original, and unformatted form," and a lakehouse "combines the best features of both data warehouses and data lakes."

  8. aegissofttech.com ·cited· (T2)

    Aegis gives the memorable one-liner: "OLTP is the engine behind your daily transactions ... online shopping carts, bank transfers, and ticket bookings," while "OLAP is the brain behind your dashboards and reports." Put another way, "OLTP records the present; OLAP analyzes the past for insights" — OLTP does "fast inserts and small lookups" and "OLAP queries, by contrast, scan millions of rows for trends."

  9. stitchdata.com ·cited· (T2)

    Stitch states the design plainly: "A columnar database stores data by columns rather than by rows, which makes it suitable for analytical query processing, and thus for data warehouses." Because "all the values are of the same data type" within a column, "columnar databases compress similar data to reduce storage," cutting both disk footprint and the volume read per query.

  10. airbyte.com ·cited· (T2)

    Airbyte details the compression that columnar layout unlocks — dictionary encoding ("replacing recurring values with compact integer tokens"), run-length encoding, and delta encoding — noting "Compression ratios of 5–10x are common, directly saving memory and reducing cloud bills." It adds that "Most BI queries reference only a handful of columns," so engines "move far less data from disk to CPU, then leverage vectorized (SIMD) processing across thousands of values simultaneously."

  11. ssp.sh ·cited· (T2)

    Simon Späti contrasts the two schools — Inmon "Emphasizes a highly normalized structure" beginning with a core "closely aligned with the source system," while Kimball "focuses on creating a Star Schema" from a denormalized core. He stresses both remain valid: "leveraging the strengths of both Inmon and Kimball's methods can be highly effective," and the real failure mode is building analytics without following "some approach (whether Kimball or Inmon)."

  12. integrate.io ·cited· (T2)

    Integrate.io explains the ETL-to-ELT shift: ELT means "load raw data directly into the data warehouse" and transform afterward, made practical because cloud warehouses "are so powerful and efficient that they can quickly and cost-effectively transform big data within the warehouse after loading" — whereas pre-cloud, post-load transformation "was slow, impractical, and expensive." It positions dbt cleanly: "dbt is the T in ELT."

  13. alation.com ·cited· (T2)

    Alation defines the modern data stack as "a thoughtfully assembled collection of cloud-native tools ... that enables enterprises to ingest, store, transform, and analyze data with agility, scale, and governance," organized into layers (ingestion via "Extract, Load, Transform (ELT)" tools, storage in "A data warehouse, data lake, or data lakehouse," transformation, orchestration, and BI). It captures the ELT shift as "load raw data first, transform as needed," moving teams "beyond 'data warehousing + BI' to a platform that supports self-service analytics."

  14. airbyte.com ·cited· (T2)

    Airbyte enumerates the stack's layers — "data ingestion, storage, transformation, analytics, reverse ETL, orchestration, observability, and governance" — with representative tools: ingestion (Airbyte), warehousing (Snowflake, "separating compute and storage, enabling independent scaling"), transformation ("dbt standardizes data transformation through SQL-based modeling"), and BI (Looker, whose semantic layer "creates a single source of truth for business metrics").

  15. databricks.com ·cited· (T2)

    Databricks defines a warehouse as "A data management system that stores data from multiple sources in a highly structured way" where "Data is cleansed, transformed and integrated into a schema" — a "schema-on-write" approach — versus a data lake, "A flexible repository that stores raw data in its native format" using "schema-on-read." The lakehouse "combines the scale and flexibility of a data lake with the reliability and performance of a data warehouse."

  16. montecarlo.ai ·cited· (T2)

    Monte Carlo distinguishes by use case: a warehouse uses schema-on-write ("cleaned, integrated, and processed before storage") and is "the most sensible choice for data platforms whose primary use case is for data analysis and reporting," while a data lake uses schema-on-read, storing "raw data, leaving the processing part until the data is read for use" — better for "data scientists seeking to run advanced calculations and data exploration." A lakehouse stores raw data "while also facilitating the option for preprocessed, structured data like a warehouse."

pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-14_data-warehouses.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
